title: AWS Credential Providers, Abort Signals, Retries, and Errors · Contract Lens: Credential Provider Chain
summary: Predict unknown input, typed state, command, stream, promise, storage, cache, security, cost, and recovery outcomes before reviewing typed request and retry state machine; use short-lived provider ownership, AbortSignal propagation, S3ServiceException narrowing, $metadata evidence, and ambiguous-write reconciliation. objectives: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, use short-lived credential providers with explicit workload identity, refresh ownership, least privilege, and redaction.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Predict Credential Provider Chain · Theory Credential Provider Chain
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 9599 during a Node stream backpressure incident. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to narrow retry outcomes and preserve AbortSignal and ambiguous-write reconciliation; keep AbortSignal and stream cleanup under one named owner; then replace one typed fixture with unknown runtime input and prove rejection before AWS use. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

For S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Defend the Credential Provider Chain model · Theory Credential Provi…, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, file, object, policy, transfer, integrity, cache, fault, cost, restore, and controlled AWS transfer evidence: Hard-coded access keys are acceptable when the repository is private. Type annotations, generated SDK shapes, and resolved promises do not strengthen that runtime claim.
